essential_question: Can you find real complementary and supplementary pairs in the world, and prove with your own hands that vertical angles come out equal?
objective: Build a physical "X" and measure all four angles to confirm vertical angles are congruent, and find and classify real-world complementary and supplementary angle pairs.

content.heading: From definitions to a real X
bullets: Yesterday: defined adjacent, vertical, complementary, and supplementary angles, and computed missing measures.
Today: build a real 'X' and measure it, then hunt real complementary and supplementary pairs.
The test: do your two vertical-angle measurements actually come out equal?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather two sticks or rulers, a fastener or tape to pivot them, and a protractor. Decide where in your house you'll look for a real complementary pair and a real supplementary pair before you start.

content.heading: Build it, then find it in the world
bullets: Build the X — cross two sticks at their midpoints (or near one end) so they pivot at one shared point, like scissors.
Label the four angles ∠1, ∠2, ∠3, ∠4 going around the pivot.
Angle Hunt — find one real complementary pair (sums to about 90°, like a folded paper corner) and one real supplementary pair (sums to about 180°, like a door against a wall) somewhere in your house.

content.preview: Session 3: defend in words why vertical angles must always be congruent, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can build an X and measure all four angles it forms with a protractor
I can confirm by measurement that vertical angles are congruent and that adjacent linear pairs sum to 180°
I can find and classify a real complementary pair and a real supplementary pair in the world

questions.prompt: Build an X from two sticks pivoting at one shared point. Measure all four angles with a protractor and record them in order around the X: m∠1, m∠2, m∠3, m∠4.
questions.answer: Any four honest measurements where opposite angles match (within a degree or two) and each side-by-side pair adds to about 180°, e.g. 60°, 120°, 60°, 120°.
questions.explain: Rubric: all four angles measured and recorded in order (1 pt) + the two vertical pairs match within measurement tolerance (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Which two of your four measured angles came out almost exactly equal? Are they adjacent to each other or vertical to each other?
questions.answer: The two angles directly across the pivot from each other (vertical angles) came out equal; angles next to each other (adjacent) did not need to match.
questions.explain: Vertical angles share only the vertex and are always congruent; adjacent angles share a side and can be any two measures that add to 180° with their partner.

questions.prompt: Record ONE real complementary pair and ONE real supplementary pair you found in your house. Name the object and each angle's measured (or estimated) degree.
questions.answer: Any two plausible real pairs, e.g. a folded paper corner split into 35° and 55° (complementary), and a door propped against a wall reading 40° and 140° (supplementary).
questions.explain: Rubric: one pair whose two measures add to about 90° (1 pt) + one pair whose two measures add to about 180° (1 pt), each with a named real object.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which felt more convincing that vertical angles are always congruent — measuring your own X, or the reasoning argument you'll build in Session 3? What did building it teach you that the definition alone didn't?
